result: Some mornings I wake before the alarm and feel the house breathing. The refrigerator hums, a distant truck sighs on the street, and in the dark I think about how many tiny systems keep a life moving. Coffee is one of them. I grind the beans, watch the water bloom, and the bitter smell tells me that the day can be rebuilt. I used to rush through breakfast, but lately I linger, reading whatever happens to be nearby: a page from a library book, a recipe on a stained card, a friend’s text about her garden. The rhythm of those small things steadies me. Later, when work begins, I try to carry that steadiness into the loud, bright hours. I answer emails like folding laundry, one corner at a time. I schedule calls, but I also sneak out to the balcony to see the pigeons strutting on the railing like impatient commuters. After sunset the city grows theatrical, windows flashing with blue light, the sky fading to ink. I make a simple dinner, maybe pasta with garlic, and let a song loop in the background. The day hasn’t been perfect, yet it has been complete. There is a comfort in the ordinary, a kind of quiet heroism in showing up for it. When I finally turn out the light, I feel grateful for a life composed of such humble, persistent sounds. Tomorrow will arrive with its puzzles, but tonight I rest, trusting those systems again and the promise of sunrise.

prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 250 words.
